content:
# Genesis Chapter 38 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 22r–22v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_038_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_038_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 36–37 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0040 | https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0041

---

*Judah Descends — The Canaanite Wife (vv. 1–5)*

**38:1** And it came to pass at that time [וַיְהִי בָּעֵת הַהִוא] that Judah went down [וַיֵּרֶד יְהוּדָה] from his brothers [מֵאֵת אֶחָיו] and turned aside [וַיֵּט] unto a man, an Adullamite [עַד-אִישׁ עֲדֻלָּמִי] — and his name was Hirah [וּשְׁמוֹ חִירָה].

**38:2** And Judah saw there [וַיַּרְא-שָׁם יְהוּדָה] the daughter of a man, a Canaanite [בַּת-אִישׁ כְּנַעֲנִי] — and his name was Shua [וּשְׁמוֹ שׁוּעַ] — and he took her [וַיִּקָּחֶהָ] and came in to her [וַיָּבֹא אֵלֶיהָ].

**38:3** And she conceived [וַתַּהַר] and bore a son [וַתֵּלֶד בֵּן] — and he called his name [וַיִּקְרָא אֶת-שְׁמוֹ] Er [עֵר].

**38:4** And she conceived again [וַתַּהַר עוֹד] and bore a son [וַתֵּלֶד בֵּן] — and she called his name [וַתִּקְרָא אֶת-שְׁמוֹ] Onan [אוֹנָן].

**38:5** And she added again [וַתֹּסֶף עוֹד] and bore a son [וַתֵּלֶד בֵּן] — and she called his name [וַתִּקְרָא אֶת-שְׁמוֹ] Shelah [שֵׁלָה] — and he was in Chezib [וְהָיָה בִכְזִיב] when she bore him [בְּלִדְתָּהּ אֹתוֹ].

---

*Er, Onan, and the Levirate Obligation (vv. 6–11)*

**38:6** And Judah took [וַיִּקַּח יְהוּדָה] a wife for Er his firstborn [אִשָּׁה לְעֵר בְּכוֹרוֹ] — and her name was Tamar [וּשְׁמָהּ תָּמָר].

**38:7** And Er the firstborn of Judah [וַיְהִי עֵר בְּכוֹר יְהוּדָה] was evil [רַע] in the eyes of the LORD [בְּעֵינֵי יְהוָה] — and the LORD put him to death [וַיְמִתֵהוּ יְהוָה].

**38:8** And Judah said [וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוּדָה] to Onan [אֶל-אוֹנָן]: Come in to your brother's wife [בֹּא אֶל-אֵשֶׁת אָחִיךָ] and perform the levirate duty to her [וְיַבֵּם אֹתָהּ] — and raise up seed for your brother [וְהָקֵם זֶרַע לְאָחִיךָ].

**38:9** And Onan knew [וַיֵּדַע אוֹנָן] that the seed would not be his [כִּי לֹּא לוֹ יִהְיֶה הַזָּרַע] — and it came to pass, whenever he came in to his brother's wife [וְהָיָה אִם-בָּא אֶל-אֵשֶׁת אָחִיו] — he would spill on the ground [וְשִׁחֵת אַרְצָה] — so as not to give seed to his brother [לְבִלְתִּי נְתָן-זֶרַע לְאָחִיו].

**38:10** And what he did [וַיֵּרַע] was evil in the eyes of the LORD [בְּעֵינֵי יְהוָה אֲשֶׁר עָשָׂה] — and he put him to death also [וַיָּמֶת גַּם-אֹתוֹ].

**38:11** And Judah said [וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוּדָה] to Tamar his daughter-in-law [אֶל-תָּמָר כַּלָּתוֹ]: Remain a widow [שְׁבִי אַלְמָנָה] in your father's house [בֵּית-אָבִיךְ] until Shelah my son grows up [עַד-יִגְדַּל שֵׁלָה בְנִי] — for he thought [כִּי אָמַר] lest he also die [פֶּן-יָמוּת גַּם-הוּא] like his brothers [כְּאֶחָיו] — and Tamar went [וַתֵּלֶךְ תָּמָר] and remained [וַתֵּשֶׁב] in her father's house [בֵּית אָבִיהָ].

---

*Judah's Wife Dies — Tamar's Stratagem (vv. 12–19)*

**38:12** And the days multiplied [וַיִּרְבּוּ הַיָּמִים] and the daughter of Shua — the wife of Judah — died [וַתָּמָת בַּת-שׁוּעַ אֵשֶׁת יְהוּדָה] — and Judah was comforted [וַיִּנָּחֶם יְהוּדָה] and went up [וַיַּעַל] unto his sheepshearers [עַל-גֹּזֲזֵי צֹאנוֹ] — he and Hirah his friend the Adullamite [הוּא וְחִירָה רֵעֵהוּ הָעֲדֻלָּמִי] — toward Timnah [תִּמְנָתָה].

**38:13** And it was told to Tamar [וַיֻּגַּד לְתָמָר] saying [לֵאמֹר]: Behold, your father-in-law is going up [הִנֵּה חָמִיךְ עֹלֶה] to Timnah [תִּמְנָתָה] to shear his flock [לָגֹז צֹאנוֹ].

**38:14** And she took off from herself [וַתָּסַר בִּגְדֵי אַלְמְנוּתָהּ] the garments of her widowhood [בִּגְדֵי אַלְמְנוּתָהּ] — and covered herself with a veil [וַתְּכַס בַּצָּעִיף] — and wrapped herself [וַתִּתְעַלָּף] — and sat [וַתֵּשֶׁב] at the opening of Enaim [בְּפֶתַח עֵינַיִם] which is on the road to Timnah [אֲשֶׁר עַל-דֶּרֶךְ תִּמְנָה] — for she saw [כִּי רָאֲתָה] that Shelah had grown up [כִּי-גָדַל שֵׁלָה] and she had not been given to him as wife [וְהִיא לֹא-נִתְּנָה לוֹ לְאִשָּׁה].

**38:15** And Judah saw her [וַיִּרְאֶהָ יְהוּדָה] and thought her to be a prostitute [וַיַּחְשְׁבֶהָ לְזוֹנָה] — for she had covered her face [כִּי כִסְּתָה פָּנֶיהָ].

**38:16** And he turned aside to her [וַיֵּט אֵלֶיהָ] by the road [אֶל-הַדֶּרֶךְ] and said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: Come now, let me come in to you [הָבָה-נָּא אָבוֹא אֵלַיִךְ] — for he did not know [כִּי לֹא יָדַע] that she was his daughter-in-law [כִּי כַלָּתוֹ הִוא] — and she said [וַתֹּאמֶר]: What will you give me [מַה-תִּתֶּן-לִי] that you may come in to me [כִּי תָבוֹא אֵלָי]?

**38:17** And he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: I myself will send [אָנֹכִי אֲשַׁלַּח] a kid of the goats [גְּדִי-עִזִּים] from the flock [מִן-הַצֹּאן] — and she said [וַתֹּאמֶר]: If you will give a pledge [אִם-תִּתֵּן עֵרָבוֹן] until you send [עַד שָׁלְחֶךָ].

**38:18** And he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: What pledge shall I give you [מָה הָעֵרָבוֹן אֲשֶׁר אֶתֶּן-לָּךְ]? — and she said [וַתֹּאמֶר]: Your signet [חֹתָמְךָ] and your cord [וּפְתִילֶךָ] and your staff [וּמַטְּךָ] which is in your hand [אֲשֶׁר בְּיָדֶךָ] — and he gave them to her [וַיִּתֶּן-לָּהּ] and came in to her [וַיָּבֹא אֵלֶיהָ] — and she conceived by him [וַתַּהַר לוֹ].

**38:19** And she arose [וַתָּקָם] and went [וַתֵּלֶךְ] and put away from herself [וַתָּסַר צְעִיפָהּ] her veil [צְעִיפָהּ] — and put on [וַתִּלְבַּשׁ] the garments of her widowhood [בִּגְדֵי אַלְמְנוּתָהּ].

---

*The Search for the Woman — The Pledge Unreturned (vv. 20–23)*

**38:20** And Judah sent [וַיִּשְׁלַח יְהוּדָה] the kid of the goats [אֶת-גְּדִי הָעִזִּים] by the hand of his friend the Adullamite [בְּיַד רֵעֵהוּ הָעֲדֻלָּמִי] — to receive the pledge [לָקַחַת הָעֵרָבוֹן] from the woman's hand [מִיַּד הָאִשָּׁה] — and he did not find her [וְלֹא מְצָאָהּ].

**38:21** And he asked the men of her place [וַיִּשְׁאַל אֶת-אַנְשֵׁי מְקֹמָהּ] saying [לֵאמֹר]: Where is the consecrated woman [אַיֵּה הַקְּדֵשָׁה] — the one who was at Enaim [הִוא בָעֵינַיִם] by the road [עַל-הַדָּרֶךְ]? — and they said [וַיֹּאמְרוּ]: There has been no consecrated woman here [לֹא-הָיְתָה בָזֶה קְדֵשָׁה].

**38:22** And he returned to Judah [וַיָּשָׁב אֶל-יְהוּדָה] and said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: I did not find her [לֹא מְצָאתִיהָ] — and also the men of the place said [וְגַם אַנְשֵׁי הַמָּקוֹם אָמְרוּ]: There has been no consecrated woman here [לֹא-הָיְתָה בָזֶה קְדֵשָׁה].

**38:23** And Judah said [וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוּדָה]: Let her take them for herself [תִּקַּח-לָּהּ] lest we become a contempt [פֶּן נִהְיֶה לָבוּז] — behold [הִנֵּה] I sent this kid [שָׁלַחְתִּי הַגְּדִי הַזֶּה] — and you — you did not find her [וְאַתָּה לֹא מְצָאתָהּ].

---

*Tamar's Pregnancy Revealed — The Great Confession (vv. 24–26)*

**38:24** And it came to pass, about three months later [וַיְהִי כְּמִשְׁלֹשׁ חֳדָשִׁים] — and it was told to Judah [וַיֻּגַּד לִיהוּדָה] saying [לֵאמֹר]: Tamar your daughter-in-law has played the prostitute [זָנְתָה תָּמָר כַּלָּתֶךָ] — and also behold she has conceived [וְגַם הִנֵּה הָרָה] from prostitution [לִזְנוּנִים] — and Judah said [וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוּדָה]: Bring her out [הוֹצִיאוּהָ] and let her be burned [וְתִשָּׂרֵף].

**38:25** She was brought out [הִוא מוּצֵאת] — and she sent to her father-in-law [וְהִיא שָׁלְחָה אֶל-חָמִיהָ] saying [לֵאמֹר]: By the man to whom these belong [לְאִישׁ אֲשֶׁר-אֵלֶּה לוֹ] I am pregnant [אָנֹכִי הָרָה] — and she said [וַתֹּאמֶר]: Recognize, please [הַכֶּר-נָא] — whose are these [לְמִי הַחֹתֶמֶת] — the signet and the cord and the staff [וְהַפְּתִילִים וְהַמַּטֶּה]?

**38:26** And Judah recognized [וַיַּכֵּר יְהוּדָה] and said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: She is more righteous than I [צָדְקָה מִמֶּנִּי] — because [כִּי-עַל-כֵּן] I did not give her to Shelah my son [לֹא-נְתַתִּיהָ לְשֵׁלָה בְנִי] — and he did not continue [וְלֹא-יָסַף] to know her again [לְדַעְתָּהּ עוֹד].

---

*The Birth of Perez and Zerah (vv. 27–30)*

**38:27** And it came to pass at the time of her giving birth [וַיְהִי בְּעֵת לִדְתָּהּ] — and behold, twins [וְהִנֵּה תְאוֹמִים] were in her womb [בְּבִטְנָהּ].

**38:28** And it came to pass in her giving birth [וַיְהִי בְּלִדְתָּהּ] that one put out a hand [וַיִּתֶּן-יָד] — and the midwife took [וַתִּקַּח הַמְיַלֶּדֶת] and tied [וַתִּקְשֹׁר] on his hand [עַל-יָדוֹ] a scarlet thread [שָׁנִי] saying [לֵאמֹר]: This one came out first [זֶה יָצָא רִאשֹׁנָה].

**38:29** And it came to pass as he drew back his hand [וַיְהִי כְּמֵשִׁיב יָדוֹ] that behold his brother came out [וְהִנֵּה יָצָא אָחִיו] — and she said [וַתֹּאמֶר]: What a breach you have broken forth [מַה-פָּרַצְתָּ עָלֶיךָ פָּרֶץ] — and his name was called Perez [וַיִּקְרָא שְׁמוֹ פָּרֶץ].

**38:30** And afterward [וְאַחַר] his brother came out [יָצָא אָחִיו] — upon whose hand was the scarlet thread [אֲשֶׁר עַל-יָדוֹ הַשָּׁנִי] — and his name was called Zerah [וַיִּקְרָא שְׁמוֹ זָרַח].

---

## Textual and Philological Notes — Genesis 38

### Manuscript Observations

**Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 22r–22v**
The chapter spans two folios. Folio 22r (page 42) carries Genesis 37 into Genesis 38 through approximately verse 18, while folio 22v (page 43) carries verses 19–30 and begins Genesis 39. The Masoretic pointing throughout is standard Ben Asher tradition. The chapter setumah (closed paragraph) divisions are well preserved. The masora parva annotations appear in the margins at several key terms. The cola and commata of the verse arrangement are clear. No significant variants from the textus receptus of BHS are visible.

**Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 36–37**
Page 36 (pa_0036) carries Genesis chapters 37–38 in continuous uncial script. The chapter header visible at the top of page 36 reads CAP XLIII (Genesis 38 = book chapter marker 43 in the Vaticanus chapter enumeration). Page 37 (pa_0037) continues with Genesis 38–39, with CAP XLIII visible at the right margin near the column midpoint. The LXX of Genesis 38 in Vaticanus is complete and legible, written in three columns in the characteristic brown ink of the 4th-century hand. No significant lacunae appear in this chapter. The chapter represents some of the most morally complex material in Genesis, and the LXX translation is generally close to the MT, with the notable exception of verses 14 and 21 (see below).

---

### Verse-by-Verse Notes

**v. 1 — vayyered Yehudah (וַיֵּרֶד יְהוּדָה) — "and Judah went down"**
The verb yarád (ירד, "to go down") here deliberately echoes the narrative of Genesis 37, where Joseph is taken "down" to Egypt (39:1: "Joseph was brought down to Egypt"). The word creates a structural and moral parallel: while Joseph descends involuntarily as a slave, Judah descends voluntarily and morally. Robert Alter observes that this chapter interrupts the Joseph narrative at its most suspenseful point (37:36 — Joseph sold to Potiphar) and the shared verb vayyered is the hinge. The phrase "from his brothers" (מֵאֵת אֶחָיו) emphasizes Judah's separation from the family — a trajectory that will ultimately lead to moral failure and then moral awakening. The LXX renders: κατέβη δὲ Ιουδας ἀπὸ τῶν ἀδελφῶν αὐτοῦ ("Judah went down from his brothers") — following MT closely.

**v. 2 — bat-ish Kena'ani (בַּת-אִישׁ כְּנַעֲנִי) — "the daughter of a man, a Canaanite"**
The designation "Canaanite" carries narrative weight. Isaac had explicitly forbidden Jacob to take a Canaanite wife (28:1). Judah's marriage to the daughter of Shua represents a departure from patriarchal endogamy. The LXX renders Shua's daughter without her name (she is never given a personal name in the Hebrew text either, only "the daughter of Shua" and "the wife of Judah"). The name Shua (שׁוּעַ) from the root ysh' or sh-w-' may carry the sense of "wealth" or "cry for help." The Vulgate reads "filiam viri Chananaei" following MT exactly.

**v. 3 — "he called his name Er" vs. v.4–5 "she called his name"**
A subtle shift in the naming agent: in v. 3 the subject is masculine singular (וַיִּקְרָא — "he called") — Judah names the firstborn; in vv. 4 and 5 the subject is feminine singular (וַתִּקְרָא — "she called") — the wife names Onan and Shelah. Some manuscripts harmonize to the feminine throughout; the Leningrad Codex preserves the asymmetry. The LXX uses ἐκάλεσεν throughout without distinguishing the subject's gender, which is natural in Greek but smooths the Hebrew distinction.

**v. 5 — Chezib (כְזִיב)**
The place name Chezib (also written Achzib, אַכְזִיב) means "deception" or "lie" (from the root kazav, כָּזַב). That Shelah — the son Judah will later refuse to give to Tamar — is born in the "place of deception" carries ironic resonance. The name prefigures the deception that will follow. Chezib is identified with Achzib of the Shephelah (Josh 15:44; Mic 1:14).

**v. 7 — "evil in the eyes of the LORD" (רַע בְּעֵינֵי יְהוָה)**
The narrator offers no elaboration on the nature of Er's sin — only the judgment. The same formula appears for Onan in v. 10. The Hebrew ra' (רַע, "evil") is the same root as the name Er (עֵר) reversed — a paronomastic death sentence embedded in the name itself. Jewish exegetical tradition (cf. Rashi, following B. Yevamot 34b) suggests Er's sin mirrored Onan's: he refused to impregnate Tamar to preserve her beauty. The text's silence is likely deliberate. The LXX renders: πονηρὸς δὲ ἦν Ηρ ὁ πρωτότοκος Ιουδα ἐναντίον κυρίου ("Er the firstborn of Judah was evil before the Lord").

**v. 8 — yibbem (וְיַבֵּם) — the levirate institution**
The verb yibbem (יִבֵּם, Pi'el of yavam) occurs only here in the entire Hebrew Bible. The noun yavam (יָבָם) means "brother-in-law" (specifically the husband's brother). The institution of levirate marriage (Latin: levir = brother-in-law) required the surviving brother to marry his deceased brother's widow if the brother died without male heirs, and the firstborn son of that union would carry the dead brother's name and inherit his property. This practice is codified in Deuteronomy 25:5-10 and is the background for the story of Ruth and Boaz. The phrase "raise up seed for your brother" (הָקֵם זֶרַע לְאָחִיךָ) is the technical formula of the institution. The LXX uses εἴσελθε πρὸς τὴν γυναῖκα τοῦ ἀδελφοῦ σου ("go in to the wife of your brother") and renders the purpose clause as καὶ ἐξανάστησον σπέρμα τῷ ἀδελφῷ σου ("and raise up seed for your brother").

**v. 9 — veshichet artsah (וְשִׁחֵת אַרְצָה) — "and he spilled on the ground"**
The verb shichet (שִׁחֵת, Pi'el of shachat) means "to destroy," "to ruin," or "to corrupt." The phrase is a deliberate act of contraception — coitus interruptus — to prevent the inheritance of the dead brother's name. Onan's sin is not masturbation per se (as later Christian exegesis applied the term "Onanism") but the deliberate refusal to fulfill the levirate obligation while enjoying its sexual privilege. The offense is twofold: social (defrauding the dead brother) and personal (using Tamar for pleasure while denying her the child she is owed). The punishment — divine death — matches the gravity of the social crime in the ancient Near Eastern framework. The LXX preserves the text closely: κατέχεεν ἐπὶ τὴν γῆν ("he poured out upon the ground").

**v. 10 — "what he did was evil" (וַיֵּרַע)**
The verb vayera' here is form identical with vayyar' (ויַּרְא, "and he saw") apart from the qamets under the resh. This verbal echo with "and Judah saw" (v. 2, vayar') and "and Judah saw her" (v. 15, vayir'eha) creates a pattern of sight-and-judgment running through the chapter.

**v. 11 — "lest he also die like his brothers" (פֶּן-יָמוּת גַּם-הוּא כְּאֶחָיו)**
Judah's internal reasoning reveals fear, not malice — he genuinely believes Tamar may be a serial widow who causes the deaths of her husbands (a concern known in Jewish tradition as "a woman who kills her husbands," isha katlanit). The irony is that both Er and Onan were killed by divine decree for their own sins, not because of anything about Tamar. Judah wrongly attributes the cause to her rather than to his sons. The result is that Tamar is left in a liminal legal state: not released from the levirate obligation (which would require formal chalitsah, cf. Deut. 25:7-10) and not given Shelah. She is effectively suspended and widowed three times over.

**v. 12 — "the days multiplied" (וַיִּרְבּוּ הַיָּמִים)**
An unusual temporal phrase — most Hebrew narrative uses "after many days" (acharei yamim rabbim) for this purpose. The idiom vayirbu hayyamim suggests an accumulation of time that is not precisely defined, pressing Tamar's situation to urgency. The occasion of the sheepshearing (gaz'az tson) provides significant dramatic context: sheepshearing festivals in the ancient Near East were occasions of feasting, celebration, and social mixing (cf. Nabal's sheepshearing, 1 Sam 25:4; Absalom's sheepshearing, 2 Sam 13:23). Judah's going up (vayyaal, not vayyered — the verb changes direction) for the sheepshearing places him in a festive context.

**v. 14 — petach enayim (בְּפֶתַח עֵינַיִם) — place name or common noun?**
This crux is one of the most contested in the chapter. The phrase petach enayim means literally "opening of the eyes" (petach = opening, entrance; enayim = eyes). Two interpretations compete:

(1) **Place name:** "Enaim" (or "Enam") is a town listed in Joshua 15:34 among the cities of Judah in the Shephelah. The LXX reads ἐπὶ τῆς πύλης Αιναν ("at the gate of Ainan"), treating it as a toponym. This reading is supported by the definite article on enayim in context and by the parallel in v. 21 ("the one who was at Enaim / בָעֵינַיִם").

(2) **Common noun:** Some commentators read "at the crossroads" (petach = "opening/junction") or, more suggestively, "at the place where eyes open" — a location visible from the road (cf. the visual emphasis throughout vv. 14-15). The Targum Onkelos renders פרשת אורחא ("at the parting of the ways / crossroads").

The narrative requires a location that is (a) on the road to Timnah, (b) visible from the road, and (c) public enough to be a recognized location. The place-name reading best satisfies these criteria. Both the EMET translation and the apparatus favor the place-name reading while retaining the literal syntax "at the opening of Enaim."

**v. 15 — zonah (זוֹנָה) — "prostitute"**
The word zonah is the common Hebrew term for a prostitute or harlot — one who practices sexual commerce (from the root zanah, "to fornicate"). It is used without any cultic overtone. Judah sees Tamar's covered face and interprets her as a commercial sex worker. The veil (tsaif, צָעִיף) is significant: in normal Israelite life women did not typically veil their faces; the veil here functions as a professional signal, not a modesty covering. This stands in contrast to the later usage in v. 21 (see next note).

**vv. 15 vs. 21 — zonah (זוֹנָה) vs. qedeshah (קְדֵשָׁה)**
One of the most significant lexical contrasts in the chapter. In v. 15 the narrator (and Judah's mind) uses zonah — "prostitute" (common, commercial sex). In vv. 21–22 Judah's friend Hirah uses qedeshah — from the root qadash (קָדַשׁ, "to be holy/consecrated"). The qedeshah is often rendered "cult prostitute" in older translations, though this reading has been challenged by scholarship (cf. Phyllis Bird's work arguing qedeshah is a separate social category — a "consecrated woman" whose sexual status is ambiguous — not necessarily a temple prostitute). What is certain is that Hirah uses the more elevated/respectful term when asking publicly, perhaps to avoid the social stigma of asking for a "prostitute" in public. This is a subtle sociolinguistic distinction preserved precisely in the Hebrew narrative: the narrator and Judah think "prostitute"; the friend asks for the "consecrated woman." The LXX uses πόρνη ("prostitute") in v. 15 and also πόρνη in vv. 21–22, collapsing the distinction — a significant flattening of the Hebrew narrative texture.

**v. 18 — chotamkha u-fetilekha u-mattekha (חֹתָמְךָ וּפְתִילֶךָ וּמַטְּךָ)**
The three-item pledge is extraordinary. The signet (chotam, חֹתָם) was a cylinder seal or ring used to authenticate documents — a man's personal identity marker. The cord (petil, פְּתִיל) was the cord on which the signet hung. The staff (matteh, מַטֶּה) was a personal staff that also functioned as a sign of tribal leadership. These items are Judah's entire personal identity kit — not mere valuables but legally probative identity markers. The irony is complete: Tamar, who needs to prove the father of her child, requires nothing less than the man's legal identity as pledge. The LXX reads: τὸν δακτύλιόν σου καὶ τὸν ὁρμίσκον καὶ τὴν ῥάβδον ("your ring and your cord/necklace and your staff").

**v. 24 — vetissaref (וְתִשָּׂרֵף) — "let her be burned"**
The punishment Judah decrees — death by burning — is more severe than the standard penalty for adultery (stoning in later law). Tamar's status as a betrothed widow (technically promised to Shelah) would classify her infidelity as something close to adultery under the levirate framework. The burning sentence may reflect earlier (pre-Sinai) Canaanite custom or possibly the higher standard applied to daughters of priests (cf. Lev 21:9). Judah pronounces sentence as patriarch and judge without investigation — the contrast with what follows (v. 25-26) is devastating.

**v. 25 — hakker-na (הַכֶּר-נָא) — "recognize, please"**
This is the exact phrase used by Judah and his brothers in Genesis 37:32 when they showed Joseph's bloody coat to Jacob and said "recognize, please" (hakker-na) whether it was Joseph's coat or not. The verbal echo is precise: the same form (Pi'el imperative + particle na) of the same root n-k-r (נָכַר, "to recognize, to identify"). The brothers had used deception and a physical object (the coat dipped in goat's blood) to destroy Jacob's understanding of reality. Now Tamar uses the same formula and the same structure (physical objects as evidence) to expose Judah's deception — the original deception of withholding Shelah, and the present situation of his own unknowing fatherhood. Judah himself was the one who told his brothers to dip the coat in blood (37:31-32). The formula rebounds on him with full narrative force. This verbal link is one of the most precisely crafted literary echoes in the entire Joseph cycle.

**v. 26 — tsadeqah mimmeni (צָדְקָה מִמֶּנִּי) — "she is more righteous than I"**
Judah's confession is among the most morally significant moments in Genesis. The adjective tsadeqah (feminine of tsaddiq, צַדִּיק, "righteous") appears in the comparative mimmeni ("than I / from me"). The phrase is a legal declaration as much as a moral one: in the framework of levirate obligation, Tamar was in the right — she was owed Shelah and Judah had wrongfully withheld him. Her action, while deceptive in method, was righteous in its claim. Some interpreters translate "she is in the right rather than I" (preserving the legal register of tsaddiq). The LXX renders: δεδικαίωται Θαμαρ ἢ ἐγώ ("Tamar has been justified rather than I"). This is Judah's moment of turning — the beginning of the transformation that will make him, rather than Reuben, the spokesman and surety for Benjamin in chapters 43-44, and ultimately the ancestor of David.

The phrase "and he did not continue to know her again" (וְלֹא-יָסַף לְדַעְתָּהּ עוֹד) closes the sexual relationship decisively. Tamar's goal was not ongoing union with Judah but the perpetuation of the family line through the levirate right she was owed.

**v. 28 — shani (שָׁנִי) — "scarlet thread"**
The scarlet thread (shani, sometimes translated "crimson" or "scarlet") tied to Zerah's wrist by the midwife echoes the birth narratives of Jacob and Esau (Gen 25:25-26, where the hand also appears). Zerah's hand emerges first, receives the scarlet marker, then withdraws — and Perez bursts out first. The narrative of the birthright reversal that runs through Genesis (Cain/Abel, Isaac/Ishmael, Jacob/Esau) finds another expression here. Remarkably, a scarlet cord (tiqqwat chut hashani, תִּקְוַת חוּט הַשָּׁנִי) also appears in Joshua 2:18, where Rahab hangs it from her window as a sign of salvation — another Sheepshearing / Judah / Canaanite connection. The LXX renders: κόκκινον ("scarlet/crimson").

**v. 29 — Perez (פָּרֶץ) — "breach"**
The name Perez (פֶּרֶץ) is from the root paratz (פָּרַץ, "to break through, to burst forth, to breach"). The midwife's exclamation "What a breach you have broken forth!" (מַה-פָּרַצְתָּ עָלֶיךָ פָּרֶץ) is a naming wordplay in the classic Hebrew pattern. Perez becomes the ancestor of the Davidic line: the genealogy of Ruth 4:18-22 runs from Perez through Hezron, Ram, Amminadab, Nahshon, Salmon, Boaz, Obed, Jesse to David. Matthew 1:3 includes Tamar and Perez in the genealogy of Jesus: "Judah fathered Perez and Zerah by Tamar." This makes Genesis 38 — the chapter of Judah's moral failure and confession — the narrative foundation of the royal messianic line. That the line runs through Tamar, a Canaanite woman who was wronged by the patriarchal family and who acted with audacity and righteousness, is deeply significant. The LXX renders: διὰ τί ἐτμήθη διὰ σοῦ φραγμός ("why was a breach broken through for you?") — reading somewhat differently, with Φαρες (Phares) as the Greek form.

**v. 30 — Zerah (זָרַח) — "rising / shining"**
The name Zerah (זָרַח) is from the root zarach (זָרַח, "to rise, to shine forth") — the same root used of the rising of the sun and, in Isaiah 60:1, of the glory of the LORD rising upon Jerusalem. Zerah's descendants appear in Chronicles (1 Chr 2:6) as men of wisdom. The Achan who troubled Israel (Josh 7:1) was from the clan of Zerah. The LXX renders Ζαρα (Zara).

---

### Structural and Thematic Notes

**The Interruption — Why Here?**
Genesis 38 interrupts the Joseph narrative at maximum tension (37:36: Joseph sold to Potiphar). Ancient readers would have felt the interruption acutely. The chapter serves multiple narrative functions: (1) It provides temporal gap — enough time passes for Judah's sons to be born and grow to manhood and die, for Tamar to wait, for the levirate drama to unfold. This gap corresponds to the years Joseph spends in Egypt before rising to prominence. (2) It develops Judah's character arc. In chapter 37 Judah proposed selling Joseph rather than killing him (37:26-27), a morally mixed act. In chapter 38 Judah fails his family duty, deceives Tamar through inaction, and then nearly executes her unjustly. In 38:26 he confesses. This arc prepares for chapter 44 where Judah offers himself as a substitute for Benjamin — the moral transformation is complete.

**Tamar as Protagonist**
Tamar is arguably the moral hero of Genesis 38. She is wronged by the levirate system's failure (Judah withholds Shelah), acts with both courage and cunning to claim what is rightfully hers, and is vindicated by Judah's own confession. The narrative does not condemn her deception. Tamar, like Rahab (Josh 2), Ruth, and Bathsheba, stands in the genealogical line that leads to David and, in Matthew's gospel, to Jesus. In each case the woman is a social outsider (Canaanite, widow, foreigner) who acts with greater faithfulness than the insiders.

**Levirate Marriage and Genesis 38**
The levirate institution (Deut 25:5-10) requires a surviving brother to marry his dead brother's childless widow. The formal law in Deuteronomy provides for the option of refusing (chalitsah, the sandal-removal ceremony). No such option is described in Genesis 38 — the obligation is simply stated as absolute. Scholars debate whether Genesis 38 reflects an earlier, stricter form of the institution pre-dating the Deuteronomic codification, or whether the story simply presents the ethical ideal without the legal escape clause.

**LXX Summary**
The Vaticanus text of Genesis 38 follows the MT closely in most respects. Notable differences: (1) The LXX collapses the zonah/qedeshah distinction (vv. 15, 21-22), using πόρνη throughout. (2) The LXX rendering of v. 14 treats Enaim as a toponym (Αιναν). (3) In v. 25 the LXX expands slightly: "she sent to her father-in-law, saying: from the man to whom these things belong, I am pregnant." (4) In v. 26 the LXX's δεδικαίωται (perfect passive of dikaioō, "to justify, to declare righteous") gives a forensic/legal color to Judah's declaration, anticipating Pauline usage of the same verb in Romans and Galatians. (5) The names Φαρες (Phares) and Ζαρα (Zara) are the forms used in the New Testament genealogies (Matt 1:3; Luke 3:33).
